page_title: Termination law and at-will exceptions — Montana

seo.title: Montana Termination Law and At-Will Exceptions — WDEA Explained | Teamed
seo.meta_description: Montana is the only US state not fully at-will after probation. WDEA (MCA 39-2-901): good cause required, 12-month default probation, 4-year wage damages cap. What every employer must know.
seo.og_title: Montana termination law: the only US state that isn't at-will after probation
seo.og_description: The WDEA (MCA 39-2-901) requires good cause for every post-probation dismissal. 12-month default probation, 4-year damages cap. Here's how it works for employers.

hero.alt: Montana mountain ridgeline at dusk — layered Rocky Mountain silhouettes with pine forests and amber horizon sky

statutory_facts.claim: Montana is the only US state where at-will employment does not apply after the probationary period
WDEA default probationary period: 12 months from hire
WDEA maximum probationary period: 18 months (employer may extend by written policy; 2021 amendment)
Good cause definition: failure to satisfactorily perform job duties, disruption of employer's operation, or other legitimate business reason
Employer bears burden of demonstrating good cause
Damages capped at 4 years lost wages and fringe benefits from date of discharge
WDEA preempts most common-law wrongful discharge tort claims
Statute of limitations: 1 year from date of discharge
Vettel-Becker v. Deaconess Medical Center confirmed WDEA preemption scope (9th Circuit)
statutory_facts.note: Case cite requires independent verification; preemption principle confirmed in MCA 39-2-913 text
